coredata.dc:title: Single step hydrothermal synthesis of carbon nanodot decorated V2O5 nanobelts as hybrid conducting material for supercapacitor application
coredata.dc:creator: Narayanan, Remya
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodot (C-dot) decorated V2O5 (C-dot@V2O5) nanobelts are synthesized by single step, low cost hydrothermal route at low temperature by using V2O5 and glucose as precursors. We have not added any extra organic solvents or surfactants which are commonly used for the preparation of different nanostructures of V2O5. Electron microscopy analyses demonstrate that C-dot is entrapped inside V2O5 nanobelts which in turn enhance the conductivity and ion propagation property of this composite material. The C-dot@V2O5 nanobelts exhibit an excellent three electrode electrochemical performance in 1M Na2SO4 and which showed a specific capacitance of 270Fg−1 at 1Ag−1, which is ~ 4.5 times higher than the pristine V2O5 electrode. The electrochemical energy storage capacity of this hybrid is investigated towards solid state supercapacitor application also for the first time by employing electrophoretically deposited C-dot as the counter electrode and Li based gel as the electrolyte. The hybrid material delivers an energy density of 60Whkg−1 and a reasonably high power density of 4.1kWkg−1 at 5Ag−1 and good cycling stability and capacitance retention of about 87% was observed even after 5000 cycles. Above mentioned results clearly show that C-dot embedded hybrid, nanostructured transition metal oxides has great potential towards fabrication of electrodes for energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Gel electrolyte
Supercapacitor
Hydrothermal
Energy density

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of C-dot decorated V2O5 nanobelt

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectra of V2O5 and C-dot decorated V2O5.
Schematic illustration of glucose assisted hydrothermal synthesis of C-dot@V2O5.
Photographs of (a) synthesized V2O5 by hydrothermal route with glucose (b) without glucose.
XPS survey spectrum of C-dot@V2O5 and deconvoluted core-level spectra of b) C 1s c) O 1s and d) V 2p.

FE-SEM micrographs of (a) pristine V2O5 nanorods (b and c) C-dot decorated V2O5 nanobelt at different magnifications; (d) and (e) are the EDX plots of pristine V2O5 and C-dot decorated V2O5 respectively.
TEM images of C-dot decorated V2O5 nanobelt at different magnifications [(a), (b) and (c)] and (d) lattice scale image of V2O5 nanobelt.
(a) Absorption and photoluminescence spectra of V2O5 nanorods and C-dot@V2O5 at different intervals of synthesis time.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of pristine V2O5 and C-dot@V2O5 at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 (b) cyclic voltammograms of C-dot@V2O5 at different scan rates, galvanostatic charge discharge profile of (c) pristine V2O5 and C-dot@V2O5 at a current density of 1Ag−1 and (d) C-dot@V2O5 at different current densities. 1M Na2SO4 as electrolyte.

Cyclic voltammograms of pristine (a) V2O5 and C-dot@V2O5 at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 and (b) C-dot@V2O5 at different scan rates and galvanostatic charge discharge profile of (a) pristine V2O5 and C-dot@V2O5 at a current density of 1Ag−1 and (d) C-dot@V2O5 at different current densities, electrolyte IM LiClO4 in PC gel.

head.ce:title: Single step hydrothermal synthesis of carbon nanodot decorated V2O5 nanobelts as hybrid conducting material for supercapacitor application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Energy Sciences and Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Science Education and Research, Pune 411008, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Energy Sciences and Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Science Education and Research

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanodot (C-dot) decorated V2O5 (C-dot@V2O5) nanobelts are synthesized by single step, low cost hydrothermal route at low temperature by using V2O5 and glucose as precursors. We have not added any extra organic solvents or surfactants which are commonly used for the preparation of different nanostructures of V2O5. Electron microscopy analyses demonstrate that C-dot is entrapped inside V2O5 nanobelts which in turn enhance the conductivity and ion propagation property of this composite material. The C-dot@V2O5 nanobelts exhibit an excellent three electrode electrochemical performance in 1M Na2SO4 and which showed a specific capacitance of 270Fg−1 at 1Ag−1, which is ~ 4.5 times higher than the pristine V2O5 electrode. The electrochemical energy storage capacity of this hybrid is investigated towards solid state supercapacitor application also for the first time by employing electrophoretically deposited C-dot as the counter electrode and Li based gel as the electrolyte. The hybrid material delivers an energy density of 60Whkg−1 and a reasonably high power density of 4.1kWkg−1 at 5Ag−1 and good cycling stability and capacitance retention of about 87% was observed even after 5000 cycles. Above mentioned results clearly show that C-dot embedded hybrid, nanostructured transition metal oxides has great potential towards fabrication of electrodes for energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Among various energy storage devices, supercapacitors or ultracapacitors have become one of the most promising candidate due to its high power density, faster charge discharge rates and excellent cyclic stability [1,2]. They have been used in wide range of applications such as large industrial machines, various portable electronic devices, hybrid electronic vehicles etc. Supercapacitor can be classified into electrochemical double layer capacitor (EDLC) and pseudocapacitor.

EDLC is considered as the electrical energy storage device with high power density, which store charges via reversible ion absorption at the electrode-electrolyte interface [3–5]. Carbon based materials are considered good for EDLCs due to their double layer capacitance which enhances the power density of the capacitor [6,7]. Even though carbonaceous materials provides high surface area and conductivity, the energy density of such capacitors is limited by the reduced double layer area [8]. On the other hand, pseudo-capacitors can provide high energy density, which are associated with a surface redox process on the surface of the electrode materials. For pseudo-capacitors fast faradaic redox reactions are responsible for the charge storage mechanism [9]. Conducting polymers and transition metal oxides are primarily used as the pseudocapacitance materials [10–12]. However, most of these pseudo capacitive materials show poor electrical conductivity and low power density. A better way to design a supercapacitor with both high energy density and power density is to construct a hybrid device that is composed of EDLC and pseudocapacitance within a single electrode.

Among various transition metal oxides V2O5 is considered as the well-established intercalation compound due to the following reasons: low cost, layered crystal structure, high energy density and multivalent oxidation states. V2O5 nanostructures such as wires, rods, belts, etc. offer high surface area, have been already used in different areas such as supercapacitor, lithium ion batteries, gas sensors, transistors etc [13–17]. As a supercapacitive electrode, the low electronic conductivity and low ion diffusion coefficient of V2O5 prevents the faster charge discharge rates. Combination of different carbon nanostructures such as activated carbon, graphene, nanotubes etc. with V2O5 can improve the conductivity of the composite and prevents the agglomeration of the metal oxide nanoparticles [18–20].

There are various reports based on V2O5 -carbon nanostructures for supercapacitor applications. All those hybrid compounds offer enhanced specific capacitance and improved electrochemical energy storage. In a previous report, graphene/V2O5 assembly offered a specific capacitance of 288Fg−1, due to the combined effects of pseudocapacitance and double layer capacitance behavior [21]. In an another report, V2O5/functionalized multi walled carbon nanotubes hybrids exhibited excellent energy density ~ 57Whkg−1 and a better rate capacity [22]. Incorporation of functionalized carbon nanotubes improves the surface area and conductivity of the composite which leads to the high energy and power density. Zhou et al. reported a bamboo like nanomaterial composed of V2O5 /polyindole decorated on the activated carbon cloth for supercapacitor application and it offered a high specific capacitance of 535Fg−1 [23].

Carbon dots (C-dots) belongs to new class of carbon nanomaterials, and these materials have attained extensive scientific interest due to their fascinating properties such as stability, low toxicity, good electrical conductivity, ease of synthesis etc. Moreover it is quite cheap also [24,25]. This class of materials are capable of showing strong luminescent characteristics, which can be tuned by tuning the excitation wavelengths. Most importantly C-dots can act as electron acceptors as well as electron donors [24]. It was used in wide range of applications such as sensors, solar cells, bio-imaging, catalysis etc [26–29]. Liu et al. had reported C-dot as the electrode materials for supercapacitor for the first time [30]. A symmetric micro-supercapacitor was fabricated by electrodeposition techniques. The results were promising for fabrication of high performance supercapacitor based on C-dots. There are also few reports based on hybrid supercapacitor based on C-dots [31].

In the present work, a facile, cost effective and single step hydrothermal method is utilized for the synthesis of C-dot decorated V2O5 (C-dot@V2O5) hybrid nanostructures as electrode materials demonstrating good power and energy densities. The nano-structured hybrid is prepared by employing V2O5 and glucose as the precursors, without the addition of any other oxidizing or reducing agent or any other surfactants. Glucose acts as an oxidizing agent at high temperature, which leads to the formation of V2O5 nanobelts. At the same time glucose is pyrolyzed and underwent reduction which in turn leads to the formation of carbon dots. The carbon dots have been decorated inside the V2O5 nanobelts which significantly improves the electrical conductivity. The V2O5 nanobelts are also involved in faradaic reactions and render high energy density. These conductive C-dots help to provide high power density to the C-dot@V2O5 composite. These C-dot@V2O5 based hybrid nanostructures has demonstrated a specific capacitance value of 270Fg−1 at 1Ag−1. The solid state device delivers an energy density of 60Whkg−1 at a current density of 1Ag−1 and a reasonably high power density of 4.1kWkg−1 at 5Ag−1 and good cycling stability and capacitance retention of about 87% was observed even after 5000 cycles.

Vanadium pentoxide (V2O5), glucose, sodium sulphate (Na2SO4), propylene carbonate (PC), N-methyl pyrrolidine (NMP) and acetone were obtained from Merck. Lithium perchlorate (LiClO4, purity > 98%) and poly (methylmethacrylate) (PMMA) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. Deionized water (DI) is obtained through Millipore Direct-Q3 system. Transparent SnO2:F (FTO) coated glass substrate with a sheet resistance of about 15Ωsq−1 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, washed with soap solution, flushed with copious amount of water and finally rinsed with acetone before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-dot decorated V2O5 nanobelt
ce:section.ce:para: Molar ratio of V2O5 and glucose is fixed as 0.75:1 and dispersed in a 40mL of DI water and then stirred vigorously for 1h at room temperature. The resulting yellow colored suspension is transferred into a 50mL Teflon lined autoclave and is maintained at a temperature of 140°C for different time intervals of 5h, 12h and 24h in a vacuum oven. Obtained products are then centrifuged, washed and dried at 60°C for 12h. The final precipitates were dark green in color. The obtained yield of C-dot@V2O5 at 24h is estimated to be ~ 76–80%. For comparison, control experiments without the addition of glucose and glucose alone also was carried out separately at 140°C for 24h.

The working electrode paste is prepared by mixing electroactive material (V2O5 or C-dot@V2O5) in few drops of N-methyl pyrrolidine (NMP). It is then coated over FTO and dried at 120°C for 12h in vacuum oven. The active material loading is maintained in the range of 1.2–1.5mg. The electrophoretically deposited C-dot film is used as the counter electrode. It is prepared from C-dot suspension by using FTO as the working electrode and Pt wire as the counter electrode. A potential of 20V is applied for 2min. This results in the formation of dark brown colored C-dot layer over FTO. 1M LiClO4 in PC gel is employed as the electrolyte for the supercapacitor. Gel electrolyte is prepared by dissolving 12wt% of PMMA in PC contain 1M LiClO4, by continuous stirring and heating at 65°C for 1h. A transparent homogeneous gel is obtained and further used for the quasi solid state device fabrication.

X-ray diffraction patterns are recorded using a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (1.54Å). The sample is scanned in the range between 10° and 80°. Raman spectra of the electrodes are recorded with a labRAM HR 800 (HORIBA Jobin Yvon) and laser excitation wavelength is fixed as 488nm. XPS was acquired using a PHI 5000 Versa Probe II equipped with a monochromatic Al Ka (1486.6eV), a X-ray source and a hemi-spherical analyzer. Appropriate electrical charge compensation was employed. The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) investigations are carried out using a Zeiss Ultra Plus scanning electron microscope for surface morphology analysis. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) are carried out by FEI Tecnai T20 with a FEG source operating at 220kV. The optical transmission spectra are recorded by using a Perkin Elmer, Lambda-950 UV/vis spectrometer and Shimadzu UV- 3600 Plus UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer. Steady state photoluminescence are measured using FLS 980 (EDINBURGH Instruments). Suitable filter is used during the measurement and along with background correction. Electrochemical characterization such as cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge discharge characteristics (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements are performed with a PAR potentiostat/galvanostat (model PARSTAT 2273) at room temperature. Three electrode cells are assembled employing the working electrode, C-dot counter electrode and a reference electrode (Ag/AgCl (3M KCl)), immersed in 1M Na2SO4 electrolyte. Two electrode cells with working and counter electrode are also assembled using 1M LiClO4 in PC gel electrolyte. CVs and GCDs are carried out over the potential range from 0 to 1V for three electrode cell whereas 0–2V for two electrode cell.

The crystal structure and purity of as prepared samples were analyzed by using X-ray diffraction (XRD). The XRD pattern of pristine V2O5 and C-dot@V2O5 is presented in Fig. 1a. Pure V2O5 matches well with the JCPDS file number 41-1426 and consistent with the orthorhombic structure and which shows sharp diffraction peak, indicating the well crystallized nature. The diffraction peaks at 25.2 (102) and 33.73 (212) corresponds to the tetragonal crystal structure of VO2 (JCPDS file no 42-0876). Whereas the other diffraction peaks at d = 3.4 (110), 2.8 (400), 2.18 (400), 1.99 (411), 1.83 (112), 1.56 (412), 1.34 (013)Å agree with the orthorhombic crystal structure of V2O5 in concurrence with the JCPDS file no 41-1426. V2O5 synthesized without glucose is yellow in color, whereas the one with glucose exhibit a dark green color, due to the existence of mixed oxidation states of vanadium. V+5 ions underwent partial reduction to V+4 in presence of glucose. Since there is no other external reducing agent, glucose is assumed to be responsible for the reduction. At the same time glucose is polymerized into polysaccharides by intermolecular dehydration and finally carbonized into carbon dots under hydrothermal conditions [32]. During this polymerization step, some aromatic and oligosaccharides can also be formed. Nucleation starts when the solution reaches a critical supersaturation and these C-dot nuclei grow uniformly until final size is obtained. Dehydration and aromatization normally reduces the functional group, but still some functional groups remain attached to the carbon dots. It is already reported that presence of carbon source under hydrothermal condition leads to the change in morphology of the metal oxides [33]. Concentration of glucose at high temperature and pressure directs the formation of V2O5 nanobelts. Eventually glucose is acting as a structure directing agent also. At the same time size of the C-dots are also controlled by the glucose concentration. Schematic illustration of formation of C-dot@V2O5 hybrid is shown in Fig. 2. The diffracted peaks are broadened due the presence of carbon content. The additional peak at 27.02° corresponds to the graphitic nature of the C-dot.

Raman spectra of pristine V2O5 and C-dot@V2O5 are shown in Fig. 1b. Two lower frequency peaks located at 280 and 403cm−1 are assigned to the bending vibrations of V=O bonds. The peak corresponds to 470cm−1 is assigned to the bending vibrations of the doubly coordinated oxygen (V─O─V) [34]. whereas the band at 520cm−1 corresponds to the triply coordinated oxygen (V3─O) stretching mode which results from edge-shared oxygen in common to three pyramids. The band at 690cm−1 corresponds to the doubly coordinated oxygen (V2─O) stretching mode which arises from corner-shared oxygen common to two pyramids [35]. The high frequency peak at 990cm−1 is due to V5+= O stretching mode of terminal oxygen atoms. In the case of C-dot assisted V2O5 shows an additional at 880cm−1, which is due to stretching vibration of the V4+= O bond. At high temperature and pressure, a partial reduction of V+5 to V+4 has occurred in presence of glucose. The color change also indicates the same. In a previous work, S-H Lee et al. reported, an additional peak at 932cm−1 when the lithium ion is inserted to V2O5 [36], which was due to the formation of V4+= O bond. A small peak located at 223cm−1 is assigned to the V-V vibration modes, which is a characteristic peak of VO2 [37]. The prominent peak of VO2 is located at 614cm−1 is not present in the hybrid, which may be due to the less amount of VO2 in the hybrid. The peaks of VO2 is masked by the strong peaks of V2O5 at 990 and 690cm−1. Some line of V2O5 is very close in position to the line of VO2 – for example 280, 403cm−1 [38] and those are difficult to distinguish.

Apart from this, there are two broad peaks at 1500 and 1345cm−1which can be assigned to the in-plane vibration of the sp2 hybridized carbon (G band) and the surface defects (D band) respectively (inset of Fig. 1b) [39]. It clearly indicates that the glucose is partially pyrolyzed to form graphitized carbon layer after the hydrothermal treatment ( Fig. 3).

The survey scan demonstrating the presence of various elements, C, O and V is shown in Fig. 4a. The valence state of the vanadium was also investigated by XPS measurements in Fig. 4d. V 2p3/2 peak spectra of C-dot@V2O5 demonstrate two peak at 516.3 and 517.7eV, which are attributed to the V+4 and V+5 components of the sample respectively. The line width of V 2p1/2 is much broader than that of V 2p3/2 due to the Coster-Kronig Auger transitions [40]. Two V 2p1/2 peak observed at 523.5 and 525.1eV are also the characteristics of V+4 and V+5 respectively. These observation once again confirms the presence of V+4 state, which was originated by the reduction of V+5. Fig. 4c the spectra of O 1s core peak of V–O–V bond is 530.51eV. Peaks at 531.34 and 533.20eV are due to the presence of C–O and V–OH bond respectively [41,42]. The de-convoluted C 1s core-level spectrum of C-dot@V2O5 (Fig. 4b) show two low-intensity peaks at 289.08 and 286.65eV flanking the high-intensity peak at 284.81eV corresponding to C–C bonds. The high-and low-energy peaks on either side of the C-C peak originate from C-O and C-V interaction. As glucose used as the one of the precursors, the oxidation of some C–C bonds to yield C-OH or C-O-C groups is feasible.

To obtain more insight into the morphology of V2O5 and C-dot@V2O5 hybrid electrodes, SEM and TEM studies were performed. Fig. 5 shows the FESEM images of pristine V2O5 and C-dot@V2O5. Pristine V2O5 has rod like morphology with a length of 1–2µm (Fig. 5a), whereas the C-dot decorated V2O5 shows nanobelt like morphology indicates that the presence of glucose leads to the formation of nanobelts (Fig. 5b and c) and those belts were aligned asymmetrically. The possible mechanism was explained in the earlier section. Carbon dots are entrapped in the V2O5 nanobelts. This actually stabilizes the V2O5 nanobelt and improves its conductivity. The length of each nanobelt is in few microns. FESEM images of as prepared C-dot shows that it appears to be fairly uniform and monodisperse with diameters in the range of 200–220nm. The concentration of glucose used here is high, and hence the final C-dots are larger in size [32]. Images of nanobelts show that they are overlapped with each other and have structural bends in some regions. The presence of the carbon nanostructures inside these nanobelts improves its conductivity and the surface area. This indeed helps to enhance the ion uptake from the electrolyte during the electrochemical analysis. TEM images also confirm the nanobelt morphology and clearly show the formation of carbon spheres and these spheres are entrapped in the V2O5 nanobelts ( Fig. 6). The dark domains in these images are from the C-dot. The nanobelt's width lies in the range of 10–20nm. The corresponding lattice scale image (Fig. 6d) shows an inter-fringe spacing of 0.58nm and this matches with the (200) plane of the V2O5.

EDX (energy dispersive X-ray) analyses are done by recording multiple spectra over different regions of a given sample. The EDX plot of V2O5 shows signals from oxygen and vanadium, whereas the C-dot@V2O5 shows the presence of carbon apart from vanadium and oxygen (Fig. 5d and e) indicates the incorporation of C-dot in V2O5.

The absorbance spectra of as prepared samples are shown in the Fig. 7a. C-dot@V2O5 shows a small bend at ~ 430nm and the deduced a band gap is ~ 2.34eV. The pristine V2O5 synthesized by hydrothermal route shows an absorption band edge at 490nm and has a band gap of ~ 2.5eV. It is the difference between the V3d valence band and O2p conduction band. C-dot alone shows a distinct absorption peak at 475nm (Fig. S2) which is due to the П to П̽ transition of the aromatic sp2 hybrid carbon. The deduced band gap of C-dot is ~ 2.6eV.

The C-dot@V2O5 shows a broad luminescent peak in the range of 420–600nm (Fig. 7b), whereas the one without C-dot doesn't show any characteristics emission peak, which apparently indicates that the luminescent characteristics arises from the presence C-dot. C-dot shows a band edge emission peak at ~ 430nm at an excitation wavelength of 370nm (Fig. S2). This value is consistent with previous reports of Peng et al., wherein, for C-dots synthesized from carbon fibers and of size range of 1–4nm, a PL peak was observed at 430 or 500nm depending upon the excitation wavelength [43]. Luminescent spectra of pristine C-dot can be attributed towards the band edge recombination. The C-dot@V2O5 shows an emission band edge at 500nm, at an excitation wavelength of 370nm, which indicates that it has different electronic structures from the other two materials.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was carried out to investigate to electrochemical capacitive performance of the V2O5 and C-dot@V2O5 electrodes. Fig. 8b shows the CV curves recorded in 1M Na2SO4 at different scan rates from 5 to 50mVs−1 in a voltage window of 0V to 0.8V where C-dot is employed as the counter electrode. All curves retain their quasi-rectangular shape, which indicates the capacitive nature of C-dot@V2O5 electrodes. The effective capacitance of the composite is due to the double layer capacitance of C-dot and pseudocapacitance of the V2O5 nanobelts. The overall current densities under the anodic and cathodic branches are higher for the one with C-dot (Fig. 8a). Conductive C-dot provides a facile ion transport pathways through electrodes.

Galvanostatic charge-discharge characteristics were studied to evaluate capacitance at different current densities. Charge-discharge profile of the V2O5 and C-dot@V2O5 electrodes obtained at fixed current density of 1Ag−1 over a voltage range of 0V to 0.8V is shown in Fig. 8c. The profile shows a small transition in the linear portion of the charge-discharge behavior, since the overall capacitance is contributed from both EDLC and pseudocapacitance [1]. During the charge discharge process, reversible Na+ ion intercalation into the V2O5 crystal structure is taking place with concurrent electron transport. The specific capacitances of the electrodes are calculated by using the following equation.
(1)
SC
=
I
×
Δ
t /
Δ
V
×
m

In Eq. (1), SC is the specific capacitance, I is the current applied for discharge, Δt is the time in seconds for discharge, ΔV is the voltage window and m is the mass of the active material of the working electrode. At a current density of 1Ag−1 C-dot@V2O5 and pristine V2O5 yield a specific capacitance of 270Fg−1 and 60Fg−1 respectively. The intercalated C-dots provides high electrical conductivity which enhance the electrochemical double layer capacitance of the electrode material. Moreover the C-dot@V2O5 nanobelts have higher surface area and layered kind of crystal structure which in turn can improve electron propagation property of the electrodes and hence increase the specific capacitance. The rate capabilities of C-dot@V2O5 are examined by recording charge-discharge curves at different current densities of 0.5, 1, 2Ag−1 (Fig. 8d). All these charge-discharge curves exhibit nearly linear charge-discharge rates from low to high current density which is an indication of balanced faradaic and non-faradaic processes. The respective specific capacitance values are 300, 270 and 250Fg−1. The electrode has retained ~92% of specific capacitance when the current density is increased from 1Ag−1 to 2Ag−1. Capacitance decreases with increasing charge discharge current density due to the diffusion effect confined the migration of electrolyte ion [44].

The two electrode studies gives an idea about device performance for the practical applications. In the present work, 1M LiClO4 gel was employed as the electrolyte and the electrochemical performance was analyzed by CV and GCD studies. The CV of C-dot@V2O5 and V2O5 electrodes in the potential window of 0–2V at different scan rates 5–50mVs−1) are shown in Fig. 9a and b. These CV curves shows a quasi-rectangular shape without any noticeable redox peak. The GCD curves also has demonstrated in Fig. 9d at different current densities of 0.5, 1, 2 and 5Ag−1 in the potential range of 0–2V. The C-dot@V2O5 hybrid device showed a specific capacitance of 119Fg−1 (223mFcm−2) at 1Ag−1 and 69Fg−1 (129mFcm−2) at 5Ag−1, whereas the pristine V2O5 based device shows a specific capacitance of 50Fg−1 (93mFcm−2) at 1Ag−1.

The cycling stabilities of the C-dot@V2O5 and V2O5 based electrodes were determined by measuring the variation in specific capacity, recorded at current density of 2Ag−1 as a function of charge discharge cycles. After 5000 cycles, C-dot@V2O5 electrode has retained ~ 87% of its initial capacitance which indicates the excellent stability of this material as a capacitor ( Fig. 10a). In comparison, pristine V2O5 based electrode has retained ~ 67% of its initial capacitance value.

In addition, power and energy densities are two important parameters for assessing the electrochemical properties of the supercapacitor. These parameters can be calculated by using the following equations [45–48].
(2)
Energy
density
E
=
½
C
( Δ
V )
2
(3)
Power
density
P
=
E / t

where C is the specific capacitance, ∆V is the potential window in volts, and t is the discharge time. The C-dot@V2O5 shows an energy density of 60Whkg−1 at 1Ag−1 which is ~ 4.5 times higher than that of pristine V2O5 based electrode and previously reported V2O5 electrode [49,50]. The calculated power density is 4200Wkg−1 at a current density of 5Ag−1. These values indicate that even though V2O5 based organic capacitor exhibits lower specific capacitance performance than the aqueous capacitor, the wide range of operating voltage of the organic electrolyte system (1.9V) contributes to the significant improvement in the energy and power density performance.

A brief summary of electrochemical energy storage characteristics with values from literature can be useful for comparing the performance of our devices with the reported results. Zhu et al. had reported that C-dot decorated RuO2 hybrid exhibit a specific capacitance of 460Fg−1 at an ultrahigh current density of 50Ag−1 and excellent rate capability [51]. The hybrid showed ~ 96.9% capacity retention even after 5000 cycles. In another report, based on Fe2O3/C hybrid delivers a maximum specific capacitance of ~ 208Fg−1. The enhancement in the electrochemical energy storage is attributed to the Faradaic pseudocapacitance of Fe2O3 and high electronic conductivity of the C-dot [52]. Yet another report by Xu et al. showed that graphene/V2O5 xerogel nanocomposites were delivering a specific capacitance of 195.4Fg−1 at a current density of 1A/g [53]. The nanocomposite hybrid were synthesized by hydrothermal method. The layers of graphene sheets and layers of V2O5 were stacked together. In an another study, based on V2O5- reduced graphene oxide free-standing electrodes were used as supercapacitor electrodes by employing organic electrolyte and delivered a specific capacitance of 129.7Fg−1 at a current density of 0.1Ag−1 [54]. They have shown that the effective exfoliation of rGO leads to the enhanced electrolyte ion intercalation. However, all these above mentioned electrodes required at least two or multiple steps whereas the V2O5/C-dot hybrid used in the present work is synthesized by a single step procedure and are capable of giving good specific capacitance of 270Fg−1 as well as high energy and power densities also.

To get better insights into electrochemical performance of the electrodes, electrochemical impedance measurements were performed for the two electrode system, over a frequency range of 10−2 to 106 Hz using an ac amplitude of 10mV. Fig. 10b shows the Nyquist plot of C-dot@V2O5 and V2O5. The impedance data was fitted to an equivalent circuit model consisting of bulk solution resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct), double layer capacitance (Cdl) and Warburg resistance (W). The resulting spectrum consists of a semicircle at high frequency region and a linear portion at low frequency regime. The semicircle represents the resistive nature of the supercapacitor and which is related to the charge transfer resistance (Rct) associated with faradaic reactions (inset of Fig. 10b). Rct values for C-dot@V2O5 and V2O5 were found to be 14.5 and 20Ω respectively. The lower Rct value of hybrid suggests that C-dot provide an excellent network for charge transfer and ionic movement. Whereas the inclined line at the low frequency region represents the Warburg impedance (Ws) corresponding to the ion diffusion into the bulk of the electrodes through the pores. The perpendicular slope at low frequency region shows better capacitive behavior. The C-dot@V2O5 hybrid shows better vertical curve compared to pristine V2O5 indicating the better capacitive behavior of the hybrid.

In summary, C-dot@V2O5 hybrid nanobelts were synthesized via a ‘single step’ hydrothermal route for the first time without any surfactant. In the past, pre-synthesized V2O5 was always mixed with corresponding nanostructure or monomer for making V2O5/carbon nanostructure or V2O5/conducting polymer. But in this report both the precursors were mixed simultaneously. As a result, it is a single step synthesis method for making such nano-hybrids as electrode material for energy storage applications. Width of synthesized V2O5 nanobelts lies in the range of 10–20nm and the C-dot is actually encapsulated inside these belts. In the absence of glucose, only V2O5 nanorods were formed. This indicates the role of glucose in the formation of C-dot@V2O5 hybrid nanobelts. Good electrical conductivity of the C-dot and the high surface area of these nanobelts helped to enhance the electrochemical energy storage of the C-dot@V2O5 hybrid nano-structured material. C-dot@V2O5 hybrid in three electrode measurement delivers a specific capacitance of 270Fg−1 at a current density of 1Ag−1. Whereas in two electrode measurement, C-dot@V2O5 with LiClO4 gel electrolyte displays a specific capacitance of 119 Fg−1 and an energy density of 60Whkg−1 at a current density of 1Ag−1, a power density of 4100Wkg−1 at a current density of 5Ag−1. The specific capacitance of the hybrid is ~ 73% higher than the pristine V2O5 based electrodes. The hybrid also shows ~ 87% capacity retention even after 5000 cycles, whereas the pristine V2O5 shows ~ 67% retention only. These values are comparable with the recent reports on V2O5 based supercapacitors with different carbon nanostructures. Moreover, C-dot@V2O5 hybrid nanostructures are economic, simple to synthesize and can be up scaled easily for production. Therefore, such incorporation of C-dots within transition metal oxide nanostructures offers a new perspective towards the next generation energy storage devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from Science and Engineering Research Board (PDF/2015/000715), DST CERI Grant # DST/TM/CERI/C99(G), DST/SR/NM/TP13/2016 and Centre for Energy Science, IISER-Pune is gratefully acknowledged.
